qa_pairs.question: How do users gain access to the Trillium cluster, including its CPU and GPU subclusters?
qa_pairs.answer: Users must opt-in on the Alliance CCDB access systems webpage (ccdb.alliancecan.ca/me/access_systems) to gain access to both the CPU and GPU subclusters of Trillium.

qa_pairs.question: Was there an automatic opt-in process for existing Niagara or Mist users to Trillium?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, users who had opted into Niagara or Mist by August 5, 2025, were automatically opted-in to Trillium.

qa_pairs.question: How can users get access to HPSS, the nearline system for Trillium?
qa_pairs.answer: There is a separate opt-in for HPSS on the Alliance CCDB access systems webpage. Users who had HPSS access through Niagara before August 5th were automatically opted-in.

qa_pairs.question: What is the SSH command to log in to the CPU subcluster of Trillium?
qa_pairs.answer: Users can log in by using `ssh USERNAME@trillium.alliancecan.ca`, which requires SSH keys and MFA enabled on their CCDB account.

qa_pairs.question: How do users access the GPU subcluster of Trillium?
qa_pairs.answer: Users should log in via SSH to `USERNAME@trillium-gpu.alliancecan.ca`, also requiring SSH keys and MFA.

qa_pairs.question: What command should users run after logging into Trillium for the first time to set up their environment?
qa_pairs.answer: Users should run the `trisetup` command, which populates essential initialization files like .bashrc and creates directories and symbolic links to scratch and project directories.

qa_pairs.question: What are the key hardware specifications of a compute node in Trillium's CPU subcluster?
qa_pairs.answer: Each CPU compute node has 192 AMD Zen 5 (Turin) cores and 755 GB of available memory.

qa_pairs.question: What are the key hardware specifications of a compute node in Trillium's GPU subcluster?
qa_pairs.answer: Each GPU compute node has 96 AMD Zen 4 (Genoa) cores, 755 GB of available memory, and 4 NVIDIA H100 GPUs.

qa_pairs.question: How was user data automatically migrated from Niagara to Trillium?
qa_pairs.answer: Any data on home, scratch, and project directories on Niagara as of July 31, 2025, that remained unchanged afterwards, was automatically copied to Trillium.

qa_pairs.question: How can users manually copy data from Niagara to Trillium if it wasn't automatically migrated?
qa_pairs.answer: Users can SSH to `nia-datamover1.scinet.utoronto.ca` and use `cp` to transfer files directly between the mounted Niagara and Trillium file systems.

qa_pairs.question: How have the home and scratch directory locations changed on Trillium compared to Niagara?
qa_pairs.answer: On Trillium, home and scratch directories are now directly at `/home/USERNAME` and `/scratch/USERNAME`, rather than nested under a group.

qa_pairs.question: What is the structure for project directories on Trillium?
qa_pairs.answer: Group project directories are located at `/project/RRG-NAME`, and all groups also have a default `/project/def-PINAME` directory with a 1 TB quota.

qa_pairs.question: Which Trillium file systems are backed up and which are not?
qa_pairs.answer: The home and project file systems are backed up, but the scratch directory is not.

qa_pairs.question: How do file and directory permissions affect sharing on Trillium's home and scratch directories?
qa_pairs.answer: Home and scratch directories have private group ownership, so users must use ACLs or change group ownerships to share files with other group members. Project directories are set up for sharing by default.

qa_pairs.question: Where should compute jobs write data on Trillium, and why?
qa_pairs.answer: Compute jobs must write to the scratch directory because the home and project file systems are read-only on compute nodes.

qa_pairs.question: What is the new software stack used on Trillium, replacing NiaEnv and MistEnv?
qa_pairs.answer: Trillium uses the CVMFS software stack, similar to other national clusters, with `gentoo/2023` and `CCconfig` modules loaded by default.

qa_pairs.question: Which module should users load to get a standard set of compilers and libraries on Trillium?
qa_pairs.answer: Users should load the `StdEnv/2023` module to get a standard set of default modules, including gcc as the default compiler.

qa_pairs.question: On which Trillium nodes is the `cuda` module available for GPU programming?
qa_pairs.answer: The `cuda` module is only available on Trillium's GPU login node and GPU compute nodes; it does not work on CPU nodes.

qa_pairs.question: Is recompilation of user code necessary on Trillium after data migration?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, recompilation is highly recommended for all code, especially if it didn't use the CVMFS software stack or had absolute paths, even if binaries were copied.

qa_pairs.question: Why must virtual Python environments be recreated on Trillium?
qa_pairs.answer: Virtual Python environments will not work due to changes in user directories and must be recreated. Users with Anaconda virtual environments should switch to using standard virtual environments as there is no Anaconda module.
